explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \because \tan 65^{\circ}=\tan \left(40^{\circ}+25^{\circ}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \tan 65^{\circ}=\frac{\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ}}{1-\tan 25^{\circ} \cdot \tan 40^{\circ}} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan 65^{\circ}-\tan 65^{\circ} \tan 25^{\circ} \tan 40^{\circ} \\ & \quad=\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ} \\ & \left.\qquad \because \because \tan 65^{\circ} \tan 25^{\circ}=1\right\} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan 65^{\circ}-\tan 40^{\circ}=\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Adding } \tan 25^{\circ} \text { on both sides, we get }\\ &\tan 65^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ}=2\left(\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ}\right) \Rightarrow A P \end{aligned}$$

content: If $P=\operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{2 \pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{3 \pi}{8}$ $+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{13 \pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{14 \pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{15 \pi}{8}$ and $\phi=8 \sin \frac{\pi}{18} \sin \frac{5 \pi}{18} \sin \frac{7 \pi}{18}$, then the value of $P+Q$ is
explanation:
The task is to calculate the value of $ P + Q $.

First, evaluate $ P $:

$ P = \operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{8} + \operatorname{cosec} \frac{2\pi}{8} + \operatorname{cosec} \frac{3\pi}{8} + \operatorname{cosec} \frac{13\pi}{8} + \operatorname{cosec} \frac{14\pi}{8} + \operatorname{cosec} \frac{15\pi}{8} $

Note that $\operatorname{cosec}(2\pi - x) = -\operatorname{cosec}(x)$. Applying this identity gives:

$ P = \operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{8} + \operatorname{cosec} \frac{2\pi}{8} + \operatorname{cosec} \frac{3\pi}{8} - \operatorname{cosec} \frac{3\pi}{8} - \operatorname{cosec} \frac{2\pi}{8} - \operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{8} = 0 $

Now, evaluate $ Q $:

$ Q = 8 \sin \frac{\pi}{18} \sin \frac{5\pi}{18} \sin \frac{7\pi}{18} $

Convert these angles to degrees:

$ Q = 8 \sin 10^{\circ} \sin 50^{\circ} \sin 70^{\circ} $

Simplify using trigonometric identities:

Use the identity for $\sin(A) \sin(B) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$.

First, recognize: $\sin(60^{\circ} \pm 10^{\circ}) = \sin(50^{\circ})$ and $\sin(70^{\circ})$.

By employing identities, simplify as:

$ Q = 8 \times 1 \times \frac{1}{2} \dfrac{1}{2} = 1 $

Combine $P$ and $Q$:

$ P + Q = 0 + 1 = 1 $

Hence, the value of $ P + Q $ is $ 1 $.

We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & 2-\cos \theta+\sin ^2 \theta=2-\cos \theta+1-\cos ^2 \theta \\ &=3-\left(\cos \theta+\cos ^2 \theta\right) \\ &=3-\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \cos \theta+\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{4}\right) \\ &=3-\left(\cos \theta+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{13}{4}-\left(\cos \theta+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

For minimum value, $$\left(\cos \theta+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2$$ should be maximum

and $$(\cos \theta+1 / 2)^2$$ will be maximum when $$\cos \theta$$ will be maximum .

So, maximum value of $$\cos \theta=1$$

Then, $$\left(\cos \theta+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2=\frac{9}{4}$$

Therefore, minimum value of $$2-\cos \theta+\sin ^2 \theta$$

$$=\frac{13}{4}-\frac{9}{4}=\frac{13-9}{4}=1$$

content: If $$\sin A, \sin B$$ and $$\cos A$$ are in GP, then the roots of $$x^2+2 x \cot B+1=0$$ are always
explanation:
Since, $$\sin A \cdot \sin B$$ and $$\cos A$$ are in GP

$$\therefore \sin ^2 B=\sin A \cdot \cos A$$

For nature of roots of $$x^2+2 x \cot B+1=0$$

Consider, $$b^2-4 a c=4 \cot ^2 B-4$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{4 \cos ^2 B-4 \sin ^2 B}{\sin ^2 B}=\frac{4-8 \sin ^2 B}{\sin ^2 B} \\ & =\frac{4(1-2 \sin A \cos A)}{\sin ^2 B} \quad \text { [Using Eq. (i)] } \\ & =\frac{4(\sin A-\cos A)^2}{\sin ^2 B}=4\left(\frac{\sin A-\cos A}{\sin B}\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Roots of given equation are always real.

content: $$\cos (x+y), \cos x, \cos (x-y)$$ are in HP, then $$\cos x \sec \frac{y}{2}$$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{\cos x}=\frac{1}{\cos (x-y)}+\frac{1}{\cos (x+y)} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{\cos x}=\frac{2 \cos x \cos y}{\cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 y} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\cos x}=\frac{\cos x \cos y}{\cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 y} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 y=\cos ^2 x \cos y \\\\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 x-\cos ^2 x \cos y=\sin ^2 y \\\\ & \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 x(1-\cos y)=\sin ^2 y \\\\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 x \cdot 2 \sin ^2 \frac{y}{2}=4 \sin ^2 \frac{y}{2} \cos ^2 \frac{y}{2} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 x=2 \cos ^2 \frac{y}{2} \\\\ & \cos ^2 x \sec ^2 \frac{y}{2}=2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Taking square root on both sides, we get $$\cos x \sec \frac{y}{2}= \pm \sqrt{2}$$

$$\therefore$$ The correct answer is (b).

$$\cos \left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}+x\right) \cos (2 \pi+x)\left\{\cot \left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}-x\right)+\cot (2 \pi+x)\right\}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sin x \cdot \cos x\{\tan x+\cot x\} \\ & =\sin x \cdot \cos x\left\{\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}+\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right\} \\ & =\sin x \cdot \cos x\left(\frac{\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x}{\sin x \cdot \cos x}\right) \\ & =1 \quad\left(\because \sin ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta=1\right) \end{aligned}$$
